episodes.title: Why cultivating agency matters more than cultivating skills in the AI era | Max Schoening (Head of Product, Notion)
episodes.description:
Max Schoening is head of product at Notion, where he’s been especially effective at getting designers and PMs to ship code, prototype in the terminal, and launch extremely successful AI products. He was previously a PM at Google, ran design at Heroku, was VP of Design (and a part-time engineer) at GitHub, and is a two-time founder. He’s one of the most AI-forward product leaders out there and one of the deepest thinkers on how AI changes how we build and use software.
We discuss:
1. What’s most worked in getting designers and PMs to embrace AI
2. Why agency—not skills—is the thing that separates people who thrive from those who fall behind
3. How the first 10% of every project is now “free,” and what that means for product development
4. Max’s “tiny core” theory of great products: iPhone multitouch, the GitHub pull request, Notion blocks, Dropbox’s menu bar icon
5. Why the SaaSpocalypse is overstated
6. Why the amount of software has exploded but the quality hasn’t, and why that gap creates opportunity
episodes.descriptionCN:
Max Schoening 是 Notion 的产品主管，他在让设计师和产品经理在终端中发布代码、原型以及推出极其成功的人工智能产品方面特别有效。他曾担任 Google 的产品经理、Heroku 的设计主管、GitHub 的设计副总裁（兼兼职工程师），并且是两次创始人。他是人工智能最前沿的产品领导者之一，也是人工智能如何改变我们构建和使用软件方式最深刻的思考者之一。
我们讨论：
1. 对于让设计师和产品经理拥抱人工智能最有效的是什么
2. 为什么成功者与落后者的区别在于能动性而非技能
3. 每个项目的前 10% 现在如何“免费”，以及这对产品开发意味着什么
4. Max 伟大产品的“微小核心”理论：iPhone 多点触控、GitHub Pull Request、Notion 块、Dropbox 的菜单栏图标
5. 为什么 SaaS 末日被夸大了
6. 为什么软件数量激增但质量却没有，以及为什么这种差距创造了机会

episodes.title: Snapchat CEO: Why distribution has become the most important moat | Evan Spiegel
episodes.description:
Evan Spiegel, the co-founder and CEO of Snap, is one of the very few people in the world who has successfully built and scaled a lasting consumer social product. Snapchat has nearly 1 billion MAUs, and Evan and his team invented some of the most important consumer products and features, including Stories, AR glasses, swipe-based navigation, the camera as the primary UX, and a lot more.
In our in-depth conversation, we discuss:
1. Why distribution is now the biggest challenge for creating a consumer technology business
2. How Snap innovates at scale with a 9-to-12-person design team: no titles, no hierarchy, hundreds of ideas reviewed weekly with the CEO
3. Why a pure software business is no longer a moat, and what actually creates durable competitive advantages today
4. How AI is changing the way designers work and why they’re now shipping code
5. Why every major Snap feature was copied and how that forced the company to work differently
6. Evan’s prediction that humanity’s comfort with AI will be a bigger bottleneck than the technology itself
7. This year’s crucible moment for Snap
episodes.descriptionCN:
Snap 联合创始人兼首席执行官埃文·斯皮格尔 (Evan Spiegel) 是世界上极少数成功打造并扩展持久消费社交产品的人之一。 Snapchat 拥有近 10 亿月活跃用户，Evan 和他的团队发明了一些最重要的消费产品和功能，包括故事、AR 眼镜、基于滑动的导航、作为主要用户体验的相机等等。
在我们的深入对话中，我们讨论：
1. 为什么分销现在是创建消费技术业务的最大挑战
2. Snap 如何通过 9 到 12 人的设计团队进行大规模创新：没有头衔，没有等级���度，每周与首席执行官一起审查数百个想法
3. 为什么纯软件业务不再是护城河，以及当今真正创造持久竞争优势的因素
4. 人工智能如何改变设计师的工作方式以及他们现在交付代码的原因
5. 为什么 Snap 的每一个主要功能都被复制，这如何迫使公司以不同的方式运作
6. 埃文预测人类对人工智能的舒适度将是比技术本身更大的瓶颈
7. 今年 Snap 的严峻时刻

episodes.title: How Anthropic’s product team moves faster than anyone else | Cat Wu (Head of Product, Claude Code)
episodes.description:
Cat Wu is Head of Product for Claude Code and Cowork at Anthropic, building one of the most important AI products of this generation. Before joining Anthropic, Cat spent years as an engineer and briefly worked in VC. Today, she’s interviewing hundreds of product managers who are trying to break into AI—and seeing firsthand what separates those who thrive from those who fall behind.
We discuss:
1. How Anthropic’s shipping cadence went from months to weeks to days
2. The emerging skills PMs need to develop right now
3. Why you need to build products that don’t yet fully work, so you’re ready when the next model closes the gap
4. Cat’s most underrated AI skill: asking the model to introspect on its own mistakes
5. Why Claude’s personality is core to its success
6. Why Anthropic’s mission alignment eliminates the friction that slows most large organizations
7. Why “just do things” is the most important principle for working at AI-native companies
episodes.descriptionCN:
Cat Wu 是 Anthropic 的 Claude Code 和 Cowork 的产品主管，构建了这一代最重要的人工智能产品之一。在加入 Anthropic 之前，Cat 担任了多年工程师，并曾短暂从事过风险投资工作。今天，她正在采访数百名试图进军人工智能领域的产品经理，并亲眼目睹是什么将成功者与落后者区分开来。
我们讨论：
1. Anthropic 的发货节奏如何从几个月缩短到几周甚至几天
2. PM 现在需要发展的新兴技能
3. 为什么您需要构建尚未完全发挥作用的产品，以便在下一个模型缩小差距时做好准备
4. Cat最被低估的AI技能：要求模型反思自己的错误
5. 为什么克劳德的个性是其成功的核心
6. 为什么 Anthropic 的使命一致性消除了阻碍大多数大型组织发展的摩擦
7. 为什么“只做事”是人工智能原生公司最重要的工作原则

episodes.title: Why half of product managers are in trouble | Nikhyl Singhal (Meta, Google)
episodes.description:
Nikhyl Singhal is the founder of The Skip, a community for senior product leaders; a former product exec at Meta, Google, and Credit Karma; and a many-time founder. He’s also one of the most honest, unfiltered voices on what’s actually happening in product management right now.
In our in-depth conversation, we discuss:
1. Why the next two years will be the most chaotic period in product management history
2. Why half of current product managers are at risk, and what separates those who’ll do well
3. Why you need to find your “moments of joy” with AI
4. The “smiling exhaustion” he’s seeing across the product community
5. The psychological barriers that prevent people from reinventing themselves
6. Why your resume’s fancy logos matter less than ever, and what matters now
7. His prediction that companies will shed 30,000 people and rehire 8,000—all AI-first
episodes.descriptionCN:
Nikhyl Singhal 是 The Skip 的创始人，The Skip 是一个高级产品领导者社区； Meta、Google 和 Credit Karma 的前产品主管；以及多次创始人。对于目前产品管理中实际发生的情况，他也是最诚实、最未经过滤的声音之一。
在我们的深入对话中，我们讨论：
1. 为什么未来两年将是产品管理史上最混乱的时期
2. 为什么现任产品经理有一半面临风险，以及那些表现出色的产品经理的区别
3. 为什么你需要用人工智能来寻找你的“快乐时刻”
4. 他在整个产品社区中看到的“微笑的疲惫”
5.阻碍人们重塑自我的心理障碍
6. 为什么你的简历中花哨的标志比以往任何时候都不再重要，而现在什么才是重要的
7. 他预测公司将裁员 30,000 人并重新雇用 8,000 人——全部以人工智能为先

episodes.title: Hard truths about building in the AI era | Keith Rabois (Khosla Ventures)
episodes.description:
Keith Rabois was an early executive at PayPal (part of the famous PayPal Mafia), COO at Square, VP of Corporate Development at LinkedIn, and an early investor in Stripe, DoorDash, Airbnb, YouTube, Ramp, and Palantir. Currently he’s managing director at Khosla Ventures. Also, he hasn’t touched a computer since September 2010 (he does everything from an iPad).
In our in-depth conversation, Keith shares:
1. The barrels vs. ammunition hiring framework (and how to spot barrels)
2. Why talking to customers is actively harmful for consumer products
3. How to identify undiscovered talent
4. Why the PM role is dying
5. The three traits of the best-performing companies right now
6. The specific interview question he asks every senior candidate
7. Why CMOs (not engineers) are becoming the #1 consumer of tokens
episodes.descriptionCN:
Keith Rabois 是 PayPal（著名的 PayPal Mafia 的一部分）的早期高管、Square 的首席运营官、LinkedIn 的企业发展副总裁，以及 Stripe、DoorDash、Airbnb、YouTube、Ramp 和 Palantir 的早期投资者。目前，他担任科斯拉风险投资公司 (Khosla Ventures) 的董事总经理。此外，自 2010 年 9 月以来，他就没有碰过电脑（他所有的事情都通过 iPad 进行）。
在我们的深入对话中，基思分享了：
1. 枪管与弹药租赁框架（以及如何发现枪管）
2. 为什么与客户交谈对消费品有害
3. 如何识别未被发现的人才
4. 为什么 PM 角色正在消亡
5. 目前表现最好的公司的三个特征
6. 他向每一位资深候选人提出的具体面试问题
7. 为什么 CMO（而不是工程师）正在成为代币的第一大消费者

episodes.title: Head of Growth (Anthropic): “Claude is growing itself at this point” | Amol Avasare
episodes.description:
Amol Avasare is Head of Growth at Anthropic, which is going through the most unprecedented growth trajectory in history—scaling from $1 billion to over $19 billion in ARR in just 14 months. Previously, Amol worked on the growth teams at Mercury and MasterClass. Before that he was a founder, and he cold emailed his way into the Anthropic role when no job listing existed. Most remarkably, he overcame a traumatic brain injury from a Muay Thai match that meant he couldn't work for nearly a year.
In our in-depth discussion, Amol shares:
1. How Amol landed his role by cold emailing Anthropic’s CPO Mike Krieger
2. How Anthropic is automating growth experiments with Claude (their internal tool called “CASH”)
3. Why the ratio of PMs to engineers might need to flip (more PMs than engineers) as AI makes engineers exponentially more productive
4. Why activation is the single highest-leverage growth problem in AI
5. Why Anthropic indexes 70/30 toward big bets (the opposite of most growth teams)
6. How he uses Cowork to detect team misalignment in Slack
7. How the company’s focus on AI coding created a research flywheel that accelerated their models
episodes.descriptionCN:
Amol Avasare 是 Anthropic 的增长主管，该公司正在经历历史上最前所未有的增长轨迹——ARR 在短短 14 个月内从 10 亿美元扩大到超过 190 亿美元。此前，Amol 曾在 Mercury 和 MasterClass 的增长团队工作。在此之前，他是一名创始人，在没有职位空缺的情况下，他通过冷冰冰的电子邮件进入了人类角色。最引人注目的是，他克服了泰拳比赛造成的脑外伤，这意味着他近一年无法工作。
在我们的深入讨论中，Amol 分享了：
1. Amol 是如何通过向 Anthropic 的 CPO Mike Krieger 发送冷邮件而获得职位的
2. Anthropic 如何与 Claude 一起自动化生长实验（他们的内部工具称为“CASH”）
3. 为什么随着人工智能使工程师的工作效率成倍提高，PM 与工程师的比例可能需要调整（PM 的数量多于工程师）
4. 为什么激活是人工智能中杠杆率最高的单一增长问题
5. 为什么 Anthropic 对大赌注的指数为 70/30（与大多数增长团队相反）
6. 他如何使用 Cowork 来检测 Slack 中的团队失调
7. 该公司对人工智能编码的关注如何创建了一个研究飞轮来加速他们的模型

episodes.description:
Read:

This is a big deal coming from Alex Lupsasca - he is the recipient of the 2024 New Horizons in Fundamental Physics Breakthrough Prize. Known as the “Oscar for Physics”, this is arguably the most prestigious prize an early stage theoretical physicist can win.

episodes.title: The $15B Physical AI Company: Simulation, Autonomy OS, Neural Sim, & 1K Engineers—Applied Intuition
episodes.description:
From building Applied Intuition from YC-era autonomy tooling into a $15B physical AI company, Qasar Younis and Peter Ludwig have spent the last decade living through the full arc of autonomy: from simulation and data infrastructure for robotaxi companies, to operating systems for safety-critical machines, to deploying AI onto cars, trucks, mining equipment, construction vehicles, agriculture, defense systems, and driverless L4 trucks running in Japan today. They join us to explain why “physical AI” is not just LLMs on wheels, why the real bottleneck is no longer model intelligence but deployment onto constrained hardware, and why the future of autonomy may look less like one-off demos and more like Android for every moving machine.
We discuss:
• Applied Intuition’s mission: building physical AI for a safer, more prosperous world, powering cars, trucks, construction and mining equipment, agriculture, defense, and other moving machines
• Why physical AI is different from screen-based AI: learned systems can make mistakes in chat or coding, but safety-critical machines like driverless trucks, autonomous vehicles, and robots need much higher reliability
• The evolution from autonomy tooling to a broad physical AI platform: starting with simulation and data infrastructure for robotaxi companies, then expanding into 30+ products across simulation, operating systems, autonomy, and AI models
• The three core buckets of Applied Intuition’s technology: simulation and RL infrastructure, true operating systems for vehicles and machines, and fundamental AI models for autonomy and world understanding
• Why vehicles need a real AI operating system: real-time control, sensor streaming, latency, memory management, fail-safes, reliable updates, and why “bricking a car” is much worse than bricking an iPad
• How open the platform is: customers can use Applied’s autonomy stack, operating system, developer tools, or mix and match with their own systems
• Coding agents inside Applied Intuition: Cursor, Claude Code, internal adoption leaderboards, and how AI tools are changing engineering workflows even in embedded systems and safety-critical software
• Cruise, Waymo, and public trust: Qasar and Peter discuss why autonomy failures are not just technical issues, how companies interact with regulators, and why Waymo is setting a high bar for the industry
• Simulation vs. reality: why no simulator perfectly represents the real world, how sim-to-real validation works, and why real-world testing will never disappear
• World models for physical AI: hydroplaning, construction equipment, visual cues, cause-and-effect learning, and where world models help versus where they are not enough
• Why robotics demos are not production: the brittle last 1%, humanoid reliability, China’s humanoid marathon, DARPA Grand Challenge-style prize policy, and the advanced engineering gap between research and deployment
• Applied Intuition’s hard-earned lessons: after nearly a decade, Peter says they can look at a robotics demo and predict the next 20 problems the company will hit
• Qasar’s advice to founders: constrain the commercial problem, avoid copying mature-company strategies too early, and remember that compounding technology only matters if you survive long enough to see it compound

从 YC 时代的自主工具将 Applied Intuition 打造为价值 150 亿美元的物理人工智能公司，Qasar Younis 和 Peter Ludwig 在过去的十年里经历了完整的自主弧线：从机器人出租车公司的模拟和数据基础设施，到安全关键机器的操作系统，再到将人工智能部署到汽车、卡车、采矿设备、建筑车辆、农业、国防系统和当今在日本运行的无人驾驶 L4 卡车上。他们与我们一起解释为什么“物理人工智能”不仅仅是车轮上的法学硕士，为什么真正的瓶颈不再是模型智能，而是部署到受限的硬件上，以及为什么自动化的未来看起来不像一次性演示，而更像是每台移动机器的 Android。
我们讨论：
• Applied Intuition 的使命：构建物理人工智能，打造更安全、更繁荣的世界，为汽车、卡车、建筑和采矿设备、农业、国防和其他移动机器提供动力
• 为什么物理人工智能与基于屏幕的人工智能不同：学习系统可能会在聊天或编码中犯错误，但无人驾驶卡车、自动驾驶车辆和机器人等安全关键机器需要更高的可靠性
• 从自主工具到广泛的物理人工智能平台的演变：从机器人出租车公司的模拟和数据基础设施开始，然后扩展到涵���模拟、操作系统、自主和人工智能模型的 30 多种产品

• Cruise、Waymo 和公众信任：Qasar 和 Peter 讨论了为什么自动驾驶失败不仅仅是技术问题、公司如何与监管机构互动，以及为什么 Waymo 为行业设定了高标准

episodes.title: AI-Native Engineering: 100% adoption, 5x search throughput, unlimited tokens — Mikhail Parakhin
episodes.description:
From running one of the most aggressive internal AI rollouts in tech to building systems that simulate customers, optimize ML pipelines, and rethink search latency from first principles, Mikhail Parakhin is pushing Shopify far beyond “add AI to ecommerce.” In this episode, Shopify’s CTO joins swyx to unpack what actually changed inside the company after the December 2025 model inflection, why nearly everyone at Shopify now uses AI tools daily, and what happens when code generation outpaces the rest of the software delivery pipeline.
We go deep on Shopify’s internal AI stack: Tangle for reproducible ML and data workflows, Tangent for auto-research loops that keep iterating toward better results, SimGym for customer simulation and theme optimization, and Liquid AI for ultra-low-latency and long-context workloads. Mikhail also reflects on PR review in the age of coding agents, the future of CI/CD, Sidekick Pulse, UCP, hiring at Shopify, and the untold story behind Sydney at Microsoft.
We discuss:
• Why Shopify’s AI usage effectively reached company-wide adoption
• The December 2025 inflection when model quality suddenly changed behavior
• Why token budgets matter, but only if paired with strong critique and review loops
• The real bottleneck in AI coding: PR review, CI/CD, and deployment stability
• Why more AI-written code can still mean more bugs in production
• Tangle: Shopify’s reproducible workflow system for ML and data experimentation
• Tangent: auto-research loops for optimizing pipelines, prompts, throughput, and infra
• Why auto-research is democratizing ML experimentation beyond researchers
• SimGym: simulating customers using Shopify’s historical data and browser-based agents
• Why customer simulation is hard to copy without years of real merchant behavior
• HSTU, counterfactuals, and modeling merchants and buyers over time
• UCP and Shopify’s product catalog as runtime infrastructure for agents
• Why Shopify is using Liquid AI for low-latency search and long-context distillation
• Sidekick Pulse, recsys, and where Shopify’s AI platform is heading
• Hiring areas at Shopify across ML, data science, and distributed systems
• The backstory of Sydney, personality shaping, and the early Bing chatbot era

episodes.title: 🔬 Training Transformers to solve 95% failure rate of Cancer Trials — Ron Alfa & Daniel Bear, Noetik
episodes.description:
TL;DR: 95% of cancer treatments fail to pass clinical trials, but it may be a matching problem — if we better understood what patients have which tumors which will respond to which treatments, success rates improve dramatically and millions of lives can be saved — with the treatments we ALREADY have!
GSK recently signed a $50M deal for their technology that also includes an (undisclosed) long-term licensing deals for Noetik’s models.  Most big AI plays in BioTech have focused on discovery, and usually result in an in-house development effort (meaning tools companies usually become drug companies). This deal stands out in that it is a software licensing deal, and represents a commitment to a *platform* rather than a *drug*. With attention on other software tools for drug development (see the [Boltz episode]( and Isomorphic for example), it is starting to look like the appetite of Pharma for biotech tools has finally started to grow.  Why the sudden interest?
Timestamps:
(0:00) The challenges of starting a biotech lab and generating data from scratch.
(0:55) Introduction of Ron Alfa and Dan Bear from Noetik.
(4:09) The complexity of cancer: Why "curing cancer" is a misleading concept and the need for new, multimodal data.
(8:24) Identifying therapeutically relevant cancer subtypes to improve clinical trial success rates.
(11:27) The importance of intentional, high-quality data generation in AI biotech.
(17:09) Lessons learned from Recursion Pharmaceuticals regarding batch effects and data design.
(20:14) Introduction to Noetik's core data modalities: Pathology (H&E), spatial transcriptomics, and genomic alterations.
(30:15) The philosophy of self-supervised learning and avoiding bias from electronic health records.
(36:01) Translating latent space embeddings and patient clusters into actionable insights for pharma.
(41:40) Using PerturbMap and in-vivo mouse models to validate human AI predictions.
(53:38) Technical deep dive: The Tario transformer-based model and auto-regressive training objectives.
(1:00:26) The GSK partnership: Licensing OctoVC and the shift toward platform-based biotech deals.
(1:13:55) Advice for small biotech AI startups: Scaling, data conviction, and lessons from scientific history.
episodes.descriptionCN:
TL;DR：95% 的癌症治疗未能通过临床试验，但这可能是一个匹配问题——如果我们更好地了解哪些患者患有哪些肿瘤，哪些会对哪些治疗产生反应，成功率将显着提高，并且可以挽救数百万人的生命——利用我们已有的治疗方法！
GSK 最近签署了一项价值 5000 万美元的技术协议，其中还包括 Noetik 模型的（未公开）长期许可协议。  生物技术领域的大多数大型人工智能游戏都专注于发现，通常会导致内部开发工作（这意味着工具公司通常会成为制药公司）。这笔交易的突出之处在于，它是一项软件许可交易，代表着对“平台”而不是“药物”的承诺。随着人们对其他药物开发软件工具的关注（参见 [Boltz 事件]（和 Isomorphic 等）），制药公司对生物技术工具的兴趣似乎终于开始增长。为什么会突然产生兴趣？
时间戳：

(36:01) 将潜在空间嵌入和患者集群转化为制药行业可行的见解。
(41:40) 使用 PerturbMap 和体内小鼠模型来验证人类 AI 预测。

episodes.title: ⚡️ How to turn Documents into Knowledge: Graphs in Modern AI — Emil Eifrem, CEO Neo4J
episodes.description:
The core argument: AI systems need more than top-K chunks. They need structured context about entities, relationships, permissions, authorship, provenance, and history. GraphRAG combines vector search with graph traversal so retrieval can start semantically, then expand through meaningful relationships. This makes answers more accurate, easier to debug, and more explainable. Emil Eifrem, CEO of Neo4J, explains why graph databases are becoming newly important in AI systems. The conversation covers Neo4j’s origin, GraphRAG, knowledge graphs, agent memory, and why future AI applications may need graph-shaped context layers.
Timestamps

00:42:05 The future graph-shaped internet
episodes.descriptionCN:
核心论点：人工智能系统需要的不仅仅是前 K 个块。他们需要有关实体、关系、权限、作者身份、出处和历史的结构化上下文。 GraphRAG 将向量搜索与图遍历相结合，因此检索可以从语义上开始，然后通过有意义的关系进行扩展。这使得答案更加准确、更容易调试并且更易于解释。 Neo4J 首席执行官 Emil Eifrem 解释了为什么图数据库在人工智能系统中变得越来越重要。对话涵盖 Neo4j 的起源、GraphRAG、知识图、代理内存，以及为什么未来的人工智能应用程序可能需要图形上下文层。

In this episode, Riley shares his philosophy for staying on the edge of any niche, why playing beats structure when it comes to content, how he runs a content operation with two overseas editing agencies and a separate thumbnail designer, and the Twitter strategy — posting viral videos across seven accounts — that tripled his company's revenue in two months. He also makes a strong case for educational screen-share YouTube videos as the single biggest content opportunity right now, and explains why using AI to write your scripts is, in his words, "suicide."

episodes.title: He got 190K Instagram followers…and regrets it?
episodes.description:
Yoni Smolyar posted a video on Instagram every single day for 508 days. Zero followers to start. He didn't tell a soul. What started as a cringe-avoidance experiment turned into a 500+ video archive, a 190K-follower audience, and a software business that made $10K in its first weekend and hit #1 on Product Hunt. This episode is part origin story, part post-mortem, and part philosophy of short-form content in a world with shrinking attention sapns.
We cover: the psychology of the daily streak (day one is the hardest), why he stopped even though it was working, what he believes about short-form video being a net negative for consumers, the app he built to solve his own screen time problem, and the uncomfortable advice he gave me about ditching my studio setup and FaceTime-ing the internet like nobody's watching.

Richard is the co-founder of 1of10, a research platform built by YouTube strategists, and his team has quietly been behind the scenes for some of the biggest channels on the platform—helping creators accumulate over 2 billion views through a repeatable, data-backed system.
In this episode, Richard walks through his complete four-phase ideation system—audience identification, outlier research (using five distinct methods), idea remixing, and validation—and backs every step with real examples. He also shares data from 300,000+ YouTube outliers on the ideal title length (hint: shorter than you think) and where the sweet spots are for video duration across different niches.
